questions.scenario: One Container Apps revision instance crashes during normal traffic. The app has enough healthy replicas and checkout keeps returning 201.

options.text: Instance failure, protected by having healthy runtime copies available.
Bad write, protected only by point-in-time database restore.
Data deletion, protected only by blob soft delete.
Regional outage, because any process crash means the whole region failed.

questions.explanation: A single runtime copy failing is an instance failure. Multiple healthy replicas can keep the workflow serving, which is different from recovering lost or corrupted data.

questions.scenario: A cleanup job deletes receipt blobs under `receipts/2026/05/` instead of only deleting temporary exports.

options.text: Data deletion, where blob soft delete, versioning, retention, and restore practice matter.
Zone failure, because a delete command always means a datacenter failed.
Instance failure, because the app process is the only thing that can delete data.
Cost allocation failure only, because deleted data is never a recovery issue.

questions.explanation: The failure is accidental data deletion. Redundancy may not give the business an older object state; object-level protection and a restore plan are the relevant safeguards.

questions.scenario: A bad release accepts orders but writes the wrong status for 20 minutes. The database is available, but the business state is wrong.

options.text: Bad write, where point-in-time restore, audit evidence, repair logic, and release controls matter.
Only regional outage, because wrong data always means all Azure regions are unavailable.
Only App Service instance failure, because app replicas can repair all written records automatically.
Only budget failure, because cost alerts prevent bad writes.

questions.explanation: The service stayed online but wrote bad state. Availability did not solve correctness. The team needs a data recovery or repair path and release controls that reduce repeat risk.

questions.scenario: Which pairings connect the failure shape to a realistic Azure protection?

options.text: Zone failure: design app and data tiers to use availability zones when the workflow needs in-region protection.
Regional outage: prepare a secondary-region plan instead of assuming local redundancy is enough.
Bad write: use restore evidence and repair plans, because redundancy may copy the bad state.
Instance failure: disable all health checks so traffic keeps flowing to every instance.

questions.explanation: The correct answers match protection to the shape of failure. Health checks should help keep traffic away from unhealthy instances, not hide them.
